speaker: interviewer
text: Batch 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia. And it looks like it's going to rain again. And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, if we could just go back to when you were still just first experiencing the changes that were coming about because of the occupation. What happened with the job that you had at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well for the time being, everything was normal. But then everybody got a letter that said service was ended, final. And we had to leave. We got a month's wages, I think. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you find any other work after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could, but didn't for a while. But then my father had friends in the insurance office. And they actually didn't need me. So I worked for a very small wages, just so I had something to. And they were fantastic people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not Jewish, but they were very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I went back there. And they helped me. One gave me a bed. Everybody chipped in, and they were very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once your parents had been taken away, you were working at this new job, were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody living with you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but that friend I was talking about, mother's house maid who used to come in, you know, still doing the housework. And she was good company to me. And then I found a friend who was a cook, who cooked for that theater, when people were assembled. And he helped me. He said, now you come tonight to the restaurant where I am cooking. And you can peel potatoes. So you're going to have to be by yourself at night. So if the Germans come, if they come to pick you up, you are not there. And you've got a stamp. You're all right. It's official. You work for me, peeling potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because we couldn't be in the street after 8:00, we had to stay there, when we were finished with the job. We just went to sleep. That's what I did for a while. And what was life like for you at this time, when you were just living on your own? Everybody had been taken from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was terrible. Yes, my girlfriend's parents were in that theater. And I brought them some food. That's what I did. Sent parcels to Westerbork. Because Westerbork was not that bad, the Dutch camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually able to send parcels to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my brother who was-- who was already in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you hear from your brother as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things would he write about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. Can't remember, to tell you the truth. It's terrible. I can't remember a lot. But I know I sent parcels to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did that situation continue for, that you were living on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until June, 1943 when I was picked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to Westerbork and stayed [INAUDIBLE]. And when I was still, then the following January.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we could talk about your time in Westerbork now, when you first arrived there, what was it like? What happened to you after your arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, happened? Registered, of course, put in barracks. There were-- I was in like the British barracks. There were Americans. Like I must tell you, I had the birth certificate, but not a passport. That's why they kept me. Because we were useful to them, could be useful to them, as exchange a British for a German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you-- they were going to exhange--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were British. There were American. There were converted people. There were Gypsies. They were all kept there, to be sent to a special camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you aware of the fact that you were going to be exchanged, or there was a chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we hoped so. Because my love was still in Amsterdam. I tried so much to go to the Swiss consulate, and more or less lived there. And they could have easily given me a passport because they were working to take care for the British. The British, they couldn't be there, of course. And after a while, the person I had to deal with, she was for the Germans. She was against us. So we never got the passport. If I would have had a passport, I would not have gone to Bergen-Belsen at all. I would have gone to good internment camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the person working at the Swiss office was a Nazi sympathizer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who was purposefully not giving people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we didn't know that, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you're actually there, were you on your own, or was there anybody that you knew in the camp that you were staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met people there. You know, become friendly with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make actual friends in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really friends, no, but acquaintances.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of you stuck together? Were you able to stay in the same barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No because of the women were separate of the men, separate barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, if we could talk about the conditions in the camp now, could you describe for me the food, and the water, and the hygiene, that sort of thing, and tell me what they were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food used to come in big [NON -ENGLISH ], they used to call it. Soup, maybe soup, in the morning coffee. A piece-- like, quarter of a bread for a couple of days. Piece of butter, bit of jam, bit of sugar, you know, rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were allowed to, if you had somebody outside the camp still, you were allowed to receive parcels. And lots of people did. But I didn't have anybody there. So I didn't get parcels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Westerbork, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about hygiene and sanitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right in Westerbork, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had enough water and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Westerbork was, you know, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of facilities did they have in the camp for you, in terms of schools, and hospitals, and that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was a hospital. And there was-- yeah, children went to kind of a school. I took a group of kids, went and played with them, sang with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What age children was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that a kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like kindergarten, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get chosen to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just applied for it. The head was a German refugee, of course. She lived in a beautiful house there. And she was like, you would say, the director.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the whole camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of that kind-- for the kindergarten. She was in charge of the children, let me put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, if we could talk about working with the kindergarten now, what was that like? What kinds of things would you do with them? You'd do singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just do games, and sing with them. Outside, always outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were the children? Were they happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they didn't realize. I used to have lots of children, one day. And I used to go, a transport in the morning, and there was sometimes nobody left. Sometimes, two or three kids left. And I had to start all over again with new kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there a chance of becoming attached to these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have much time for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like every week, they went a transport. So you didn't have longer than a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these children with their parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of supplies did you have in the kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing, just the games with them, and sing songs. And at one time, I was-- you know, in Holland, we have Santa Klaus and Black Peter. At one time, you know, I was Black Peter at one stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they recognized me from the jumper I had on under the cloak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what else, what other kinds of things like that? Any other special occasions that you can remember from that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, then I got sick. I got what they call now hepatitis. And it was yellow jaundice, they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I got special rations of sugar and jam because you had to have lots of sweet things. And then they were talking about, we were going to a very good camp, to Bergen-Belsen. And we were going in a beautiful train, in a passenger train. And we got food to go with us. But I wasn't allowed to go because I was recovering from the yellow jaundice. And I just could cry because, you know, I missed that. I missed the good camp. And they had the train came. The food went in it. And well I wasn't to go. But the train didn't go either. The train came and left. And then maybe a week later, it came again. And then we went in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the train that had pulled up in the first instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why they did it. But what held it up-- I don't know. Maybe just to pester us, or maybe there was some difficulty. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say to pester you, were there other instances where they were trying to annoy you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, no, not really. But you could expect anything of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can just talk a little bit more about Westerbork before we get to Bergen-Belsen-- Bergen-Belsen, what was the structure of the camp like, in terms of the hierarchy? Were you in charge of anything else, in terms of the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had a [GERMAN] there. Every barrack had somebody who was in charge. And that was usually one of the German refugees. Because they lived in the houses. They were the friends, friends of the Germans. And they stayed until last. They helped us to go to the camps, to Germany and Poland. And they stayed on until last. Because they were the old ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean the friends of the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans put them in charge. None of us would be in charge. Everything was in the hands of those German Jewish refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were actually given--

speaker: interviewee
text: Preference.

speaker: interviewer
text: --preference by the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, by the Germans. They lived not in barracks. They had their own houses. And it was built by the Dutchmen, in 1936, to house them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. But they were bossy. And there was a bit of, you know, hate, hatred. Because we said, look, we did everything for them. And now they do that to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I would just accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else would they do, in terms of organizing the camp? Were there any cultural activities going on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no theater or music?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about attempts to keep up religious observances at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did it on their own, whatever they wanted to do. Because there were rabbis, you know, and very religious people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't participate in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were families together, so what they wanted to do they did amongst them. But not-- not organized. Not organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it actually permitted, or did it have to be underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK and are there any people that you can remember in particular, from your time in Westerbork, any prominent personalities at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. But they all, most of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was basically a transit camp then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right is there anything else that you want to mention from your time in Westerbork, anything that stands out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from Westerbork. I know that at one stage, my parents in law came, stayed one day. And they went on the train. And an auntie of my husband, fiancee, I shared a bed with her at one stage. She went in the train. I saw everybody go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Really terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To the east, to Poland, to work camp. But not gas chambers, no, we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans try to tell you different things?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't tell us anything. But we knew it was not good. We'd rather stay in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other jobs while you were at Westerbork, apart from working in the kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, carrying sand from one end to the other end, and back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just passed the time, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that very difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you stayed, you had to do it. I remember also where, read beans. They call it reading, you know, sorting them out, beans. Maybe for factory or something. I did that for a little while. That's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your brother? Did he also have to do these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think he did anything. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a couple of years younger than I was. But he was not quite-- how would I say-- handicapped. Not normal, but not bad either. Maybe these days they would call it ADD, you know? Attention deficiency. These days, they would have been able to help him. But those days-- that's why he died. He wouldn't stand to camp life, missed the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Westerbork, and later in Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so did you feel a special kind of responsibility towards him, to look after him then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you do, in terms of looking after him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing really, just keep an eye on him. But couldn't do much. Because he couldn't keep a secret Well, you know, he'd blabber.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of secrets?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, anything. It's-- it's very difficult. He had friends that tried to look after. The men there helped him shave. You know, they helped to look after him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like in the camp, in terms of the other people, in terms of trusting them? Did you feel that you could trust them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, you could trust each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the different nationalities that were there? And how many different nationalities were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not different nationalities-- nothing-- not in Westerbork. There was just Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Dutch and the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of course, there was a bit of a hate for the Germans, even though they were Jewish. Because they had preference above us. And we didn't like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any actual conflict?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you actually in Westerbork for?

speaker: interviewee
text: From June '43 until January '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me about that? What happened once you actually had gotten on the train to Bergen-Belsen? What was the train like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passenger train, it was all right. And then, when we arrived, we thought, a good camp. And straightaway, we saw the SS, with the cross and bone, that regiment. And I remember the 7 o'clock in the morning, far away, we heard a church bell. I'll never forget that. It was so quiet. And there was a truck standing ready. And we had a nurse on the train. And she said, I've got people who had been sick. And I was sick. And I want them to go on the truck. Because the others had to walk for a mile to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got on the truck. And I had a girlfriend. She had a little boy. And she said, you take him. You take him, So I don't have to carry him. Little did we know, had that happened in one of the other camps, that whole truck would have gone straight to the gas chambers, that little boy as well. But there were no gas chambers in Belsen. So it ended up all right. I came in the camp. I went straightaway in a hospital. They made a hospital available for us. I was there a few days. And then I came out. Could you tell me about the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it wasn't much. It wasn't ready. It was just a barrack with a few beds. But lucky, you know, I wasn't very sick. I was only recuperating.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it compare to the hospital that had been in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Westerbork was better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of equipment and facilities had they had in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Belsen they had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they called it a revere, the hospital. They had a doctor, a very good doctor, Greek. Because the Greek were there already, Greek Jews. And the head of the camp was Greek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the actual structure of the camp like in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: All barracks. All barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it for me? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was out in the middle of a forest. You know, you could see trees. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very, very big. And when you went out of the gate, you know, when people who worked outside the camp had to go out of the gate, and they would walk for miles. And then there was another camp, but I don't know what was behind. Political prisoners, Frenchmen, and all different-- Belsen was very, very big. It had lot of small camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how much contact did you have with the other prisoners who were in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know, at the time, how the camp was actually structured, in terms of the different types of prisoners who were in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the name of the section that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it all Dutch Jews who were together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get on together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, hold on a minute. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah I think so. I think so anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get on together? Did you feel a special bond because you were all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well as I said, my friend gave me the little boy. I looked after him for a while, while she worked in the kitchen, which was outside the camp. And I did laundry in the camp for all the old people's home. That way, I could look after him. As she could smuggle out some food sometimes. Very dangerous, but she did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. On the body, in between two scarves and different ways. She was very clever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of food would she smuggle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread, bit of sausage. I can't even remember. But she did it. And that all for the child. I got some.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if we could talk about the conditions in Belsen now, how did that compare to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The toilets, if you can call it toilets, was a long row of holes. You sat back to back, next to each other. The latrine, as they called it. Once in a while, we had to go to the baths, which was outside the camp. We had a German to come with us. And we had to undress in front of him in one room, walk to the showers naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it just women? Or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, just women. But you know, old women children, anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you feel about privacy at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. In the beginning, the first few times, you felt terrible. You walked with a towel like this. But later on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just wasn't so important?

speaker: interviewee
text: That didn't matter. Didn't matter at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about food? How much food did you get each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what you--

speaker: interviewee
text: About similar to Westerbork. In the morning, you got something that looked like coffee. But what it was, I don't know. Some surrogate. We got a piece of bread, for a few days, your ration of sugar, your ration of butter. And midday was soup again. And there was one kind of soup. And we loved it. Oh, tonight it's bread-- bread soup. And it looked brown and thick. And oh, it was lovely. It was sweet. And when everybody had their share, and there was left, you got numbers. You know, you got a second portion. The next day, got a second portion. And we all said, oh, when we're home, we'll come to make the soup. It was one of our favorites. God knows what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so were you hungry over this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but you got used to it. While lying in bed, we were going to tell all kinds of recipes to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of recipes would you tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it something which was on your mind all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was on your mind all the time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the priorities for you at the time that you were in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was most important to you overall? Was food one of the things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We didn't think further than the day we lived in. We didn't know what was going to happen. And we didn't care. And we were numb, kind of. You know? You didn't care. You just lived. I had a call it-- zombie, kind of zombie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to go on roll call in the morning. And they were counting, and counting, and counting again. There was one German. His name was William. And we used to call him William Tell, you know, because he was counting. That word, tell, means counting. And they probably were one short. And they counted, and counted, and counted. And we stood there, sometimes in the snow and ice, and the cold. Eventually it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after roll calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had go to work, who had a job. Men outside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about work in a second. But first of all, how much did you actually get to enjoy anything at all of time in the camp? Was there any enjoyment whatsoever? Or was it all bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, we were numb. You know? Anything was good. Anything was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you'd have been keeping a diary at the time, what are the things that you think you would have written about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would have been the same every day. Nothing ever happened, until I got typhoid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, if we could just talk about the jobs that you had. So you were working in a laundry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, if you call it laundry. It was just in the wash house where everybody washed themselves in cold water. But we tried to keep very clean. Everybody washing all, every morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had access to washing conditions every morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, cold water and the toilets were in there too. And they were just overflowing sometimes. Since we were washing ourselves, we stood in the dirt, you might say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were doing the actual washing, was that for any barracks in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for the old people's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of contact would you have with the old people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. It's terrible, but I can't remember. I'm sorry. But I know I did washing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do that for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long, but then I went in the shoe commando.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You got a whole heap of old shoes and boots. And we had gotten a knife. Everything where it was stitched, undo the stitching. So you ended up with pieces of leather. That's what we had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did the Germans tell you about where these shoes had come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't tell us anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't ask. And we didn't know. And we didn't think. That was the job we had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an idea of the numbers of shoes that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Heaps, heaps. Now after the war, we realized, you know, they were from the people who were murdered. But we didn't think so far ahead at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about, were you aware of other commandos of a similar kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: We also had, at one stage, they had-- they called them bread bags, little bags that probably where they-- if they went out to work, and I had to carry the lunch in. And apparently, they were damaged with a bomb raid and waterlogged. And we had to sort them out, the good ones and the bad ones. And I remember some of the women made bras of them. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just end it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two, of the interview with Charlotte Melkmen. It's the 24th of March, 1995. We were in Sydney, Australia.
